Glossary About
Antiquity

Aristotle

384 – 322 BCE

Soul as the form of a living body: hylomorphism, faculties, and the beginnings of a scientific psychology.

Aristotle on the soul: a primary-source report

What follows is deliberately primary-source-forward. I am relying as much as possible on Aristotle’s own extant treatises rather than on modern commentators, and I am taking your attached Plato report only as the baseline for comparison between Plato and Aristotle. Because Aristotle does not confine “the soul” to one isolated text, the best way to read his position is synoptically: De Anima gives the ontology and basic powers of soul, the Parva Naturalia show how those powers operate in embodied life, and the Nicomachean Ethics studies the same soul under the aspects of character, choice, happiness, and contemplation. Aristotle himself effectively licenses this distribution: in De Anima he says soul is “in some sense the principle of animal life”; in De Sensu he says sensation, memory, appetite, pleasure, pain, waking, sleeping, life, and death are attributes of soul and body together; and in the Ethics he says the student of politics must know the soul “somehow,” because happiness is an activity of soul.

Aristotle’s most basic claim is that soul is not an extra thing lodged inside a living body as one object is lodged inside another. In De Anima II.1 his classical definition is that soul is the first actuality of a natural organized body having life potentially. The body therefore is not the soul; but neither is the soul a body. Their unity is like wax and its imprint, or like sight and the eye: when sight is gone, the “eye” remains only in name. Metaphysics VII and Parts of Animals make the same point from another angle: the soul is the form or substance of the living being, so that a dead finger or dead hand is finger or hand only equivocally.

That already marks Aristotle’s decisive distance from the Platonic baseline summarized in your attached report. In that report, Plato’s ψυχή is repeatedly presented as more readily separable from body, more overtly immortal, and in some dialogues prior to body as a self-moving or world-ordering principle. Aristotle does not abandon the thought that the soul is what most matters in us, or that the better element should rule the worse. But he relocates those thoughts within a hylomorphic biology: soul is what makes a living body to be the kind of living body it is. Relative to Plato, Aristotle’s characteristic move is not to deny soul but to immanentize it.

1. De Anima: what soul is

De Anima I begins not with a myth of the soul’s fate after death but with the problem of how soul can be studied scientifically. Aristotle says that most affections usually assigned to soul—anger, courage, appetite, sensation, gentleness, fear, pity, joy, love, hatred—are not affections of soul by itself but of the ensouled composite. Their definitions must therefore include bodily material, causal conditions, and telos. Anger, for example, cannot be exhausted by a thin dialectical formula like “desire for retaliation”; it is also a movement of a certain body, or part of a body, under certain conditions and for a certain end. This is why the study of soul belongs, at least to a large extent, to natural science.

Aristotle does leave a small opening. If any affection were proper to soul alone, separability might be thinkable, and thought looks like the most plausible candidate. But even here he is cautious, because ordinary human thinking seems to involve imagination, and imagination is inseparable from embodied life. So the working presumption of De Anima is anti-separatist: psychology is principally the science of an embodied living thing, not the science of a detachable inner substance.

From the definition in Book II several consequences follow at once. First, soul is inseparable from body at least in many of its powers, because the actuality of those powers just is the actuality of bodily organs. Second, the old question whether soul and body are “one” is badly posed; matter and form are one composite, not two self-standing substances awkwardly joined. Third, soul is cause of the living body in all three principal Aristotelian senses: as formal cause, as final cause, and as source of movement. Natural bodies are organs “for the sake of” soul.

Aristotle’s preferred way of differentiating soul is therefore not first by Platonic “parts” but by powers or faculties. In De Anima II he enumerates nutritive, appetitive, sensitive, locomotive, and thinking powers. Plants have the nutritive alone; animals add sensation and therefore appetite; some add locomotion; only a limited class possess intellect. The structure is hierarchical rather than competitive: higher forms of life include lower powers without abolishing them. This is already a major difference from a purely ethical-political partition of soul. Aristotle’s psychology begins from forms of life.

The nutritive or reproductive soul is not an afterthought. Aristotle calls it the most primitive and widely distributed power of soul. Its characteristic acts are nutrition, growth, decay, and reproduction. The striking teleological thought is that each living thing, so far as its nature allows, generates another like itself in order to “partake in the eternal and divine” as far as a perishable thing can. Since no perishable individual can remain numerically the same forever, it preserves itself specifically, through offspring. Aristotle retains something like a Platonic orientation toward the divine, but he relocates it from personal immortality to natural generation and continuity of form.

Nutrition also shows how fully Aristotle integrates soul into biology. Life is not only thinking and choosing but metabolizing and maintaining oneself. In De Anima he treats warmth as the instrument through which food is “concocted”; in the biological works he links the persistence of life to connate heat and makes the nutritive source central to the organism. Soul is therefore not merely the principle of cognition; it is what makes growth, self-maintenance, and reproduction possible.

Perception (aisthēsis) is the next great threshold. Aristotle’s classic formula is that sense receives the sensible form without the matter, like wax receiving the imprint of a seal without taking the bronze or gold itself. The sense faculty is potentially what the sensible object makes it actually. Perception is therefore neither a copy floating in a separately existing soul nor a crude material transfer of chunks of object into organ; it is an actualization of a capacity through form. Aristotle also distinguishes special sensibles proper to one sense, common sensibles such as movement, rest, number, figure, and magnitude, and incidental sensibles apprehended only concomitantly.

Among the senses, touch has a privileged status because every animal has it and no animal can exist without it. Aristotle ties touch to food and thus to animality itself. Excess in visible or audible objects may damage a particular sense organ, but excess in tangible qualities can destroy the animal outright, because touch concerns the qualities on which bodily life depends. The organ of touch, like every sense organ, must stand in a mean between contraries; where there is no such mean, as in plants, there is no perception. At this point psychology is inseparable from zoology.

Between perception and thought Aristotle places phantasia, imagination or presentation. It is not identical with perception, knowledge, or belief, though it arises from actual perception and can persist after perception has ceased. Dreaming proves that images remain active when the external senses are not. Because animals often move under the guidance of images, and because human beings too act from imagination when intellect is obscured by passion, disease, or sleep, phantasia becomes the bridge between sense and action. Aristotle’s psychology is therefore not a simple two-level model of body plus reason; it is an articulated economy of perception, image, appetite, and movement.

Even intellect (nous) is not, in ordinary human operation, detached from these lower levels. Aristotle says thought is like perception in being a discriminative reception, but unlike perception because thought can be false where perception of a special sensible is not. Most important, he says flatly that the soul never thinks without an image: to the thinking soul, images function as if they were objects of perception. This is one of Aristotle’s deepest departures from the Platonic strand that treats the soul as knowing best by turning away from the sensible. For Aristotle, at least in ordinary human cognition, thought works with phantasmata.

Yet Aristotle does not reduce intellect to imagination. In De Anima III.4–5 he distinguishes a mind that is potentially all things from a mind that makes all things actual, comparing the latter to light, which makes potentially visible colours actually visible. He thus introduces within intellect a receptive aspect and an active or productive aspect. The active intellect receives the strongest predicates in the whole treatise: it is separable, impassible, unmixed, activity in essence, and—when set free—“immortal and eternal,” whereas the passive side is destructible.

Textually, the safest conclusion is not that Aristotle simply reproduces a Platonic doctrine of the soul’s personal immortality. What he explicitly calls immortal and eternal is this active intellect, not the whole embodied complex of memory, imagination, appetite, and character. He immediately adds that “we do not remember” its former activity because memory belongs to the destructible side; and On Memory in any case roots remembering in the primary perceptive faculty and in time. So Aristotle leaves a real separability thesis for intellect, but a far thinner one than the Platonic post-mortem biography of the soul described in your Plato report.

2. The Parva Naturalia: soul in embodied operation

The Parva Naturalia make explicit what De Anima already implied: most psychic life is studied best not in abstraction from the body but in the soul-body composite. At the opening of De Sensu Aristotle says that sensation, memory, passion, appetite, desire, pleasure, pain, waking, sleeping, youth, old age, life, and death are attributes of soul and body together, and that sensation is generated “in the soul through the medium of the body.” These shorter treatises are not marginal appendices. They are the place where Aristotelian psychology becomes concrete.

De Sensu expands the analysis of perception in two especially important ways. First, Aristotle asks how one and the same animal perceives a complex object—say something white and sweet—as one thing. His answer is that there must be some one faculty in the soul that perceives all percepts, although each genus of sensible is received through a different organ. Second, he argues that a single sensory act cannot actualize two distinct sensibles at once unless they are unified in one perceived object. He is trying to show how a unified perceiver can discriminate plurality without splitting into multiple inner spectators.

The same treatise also treats perception more positively than a radically anti-bodily psychology would allow. Senses are given for preservation in mobile animals, but in intelligent animals they also bring into the soul the differentiations from which speculative and practical truth arise. Aristotle is thus unlike any reading of Plato’s Phaedo on which perception is chiefly contamination. Perception does not yet yield science, but it is the indispensable beginning from which science eventually grows.

On Memory and Reminiscence pushes this naturalization further. Memory is neither perception nor conception simply speaking, but a state or affection of one of these conditioned by lapse of time. What memory remembers is the past, not the present or future. Only beings that perceive time can remember, and many nonhuman animals do. If memory belonged to pure intellect, Aristotle says, lower animals would not have it. Memory therefore belongs directly to the primary perceptive faculty and only incidentally to intellect.

Aristotle’s analysis of the mnemonic image is subtle. The presentation left by perception is like an impression or picture stamped into the soul-body complex. Remembering is not merely having that image present; it is apprehending the image as a likeness of something formerly perceived or thought. That is why one may have an image without recognizing it as memory, or mistake a mere phantasm for a recollection. Aristotle here builds a rigorously temporal account: to remember is to have a present image under the aspect of “formerly.”

Recollection (anamnesis) is therefore not for Aristotle what it often is for Plato. It is not the soul’s recovery of prenatal acquaintance with Forms, as your attached Plato report emphasizes in its treatment of the Meno and Phaedo. Aristotle says recollection belongs only to human beings and is a kind of search or quasi-inference, a tracing through ordered associations until the forgotten item is recovered. The contrast is sharp: Plato makes recollection evidence of the soul’s pre-bodily knowledge; Aristotle makes it an embodied, temporal retrieval from experience.

On Sleep and Sleeplessness treats sleep and waking as affections of the same part of the animal, namely the common sensory capacity. Sleep is a kind of inhibition or tying-up of the sense-perceptive faculty; all animals sleep because all animals have sensation, while plants do not because they lack it. Aristotle also insists that sleep is necessary and exists for the sake of conservation. It is tied to nutrition, the movement of heat, and the state of the primary sense-organ. Sleep is not a mystical liberation of soul from body, but a physiological recession of ordinary sensory activity.

On Dreams then asks to which faculty dream belongs. Aristotle’s answer is that dream is not actual perception and not bare judgement or science as such; it is a phantasma occurring in sleep. Residual sensory motions continue after waking perception has ceased, and in sleep, when external correction is absent, those residual motions present themselves as dreams. The explanatory center remains naturalistic. Dreaming is not evidence that the soul has become fully independent of the body; it is a special case of the after-effect of perception.

Even so-called prophetic dreams are treated in the same restrained way. In On Prophesying by Dreams Aristotle says dreams may be causes, tokens, or mere coincidences. Many are coincidences. Others arise because sleep quiets larger disturbances and allows minute bodily changes—the beginnings of disease, for example—to become more noticeable. Whatever room he leaves for marvel, the explanatory center is natural sensitivity, not inspired revelation.

On Youth and Old Age, On Life and Death, and related biological works show how far Aristotle is willing to carry this psychophysiology. He says the essential reality of soul is not corporeal, yet it must be present in some bodily part that governs the members. In sanguineous animals he places the source of nutritive and sensitive soul in the heart and locates the common sensorium there as well. Life depends on the maintenance of connate heat; death is the destruction or extinction of that heat. The physiology is cardiocentric rather than encephalocentric, but the philosophical point remains constant: psychic powers are realized only in an organized living body.

3. The Nicomachean Ethics: soul as the subject of virtue and happiness

When Aristotle turns to ethics, he does not leave psychology behind. Nicomachean Ethics I says that the student of politics must know the facts about soul “somehow,” just as a physician must know the body, because happiness is an activity of soul and virtue is human virtue, that is, virtue of soul rather than body. Ethical theory therefore presupposes psychology, though not in the full technical detail required by De Anima. The Ethics offers a practical abridgement of Aristotle’s broader science of soul.

The opening of the Ethics is already a development away from Platonism. Aristotle explicitly criticizes the Platonic Form of the Good, arguing that “good” is said in many ways and cannot be one separate universal standing over all goods. Accordingly, the human good is not participation in a transcendent Good itself but the fulfillment of a human function. That function is an activity of soul that follows or implies a rational principle; happiness therefore becomes activity of soul in accordance with virtue, and with the best and most complete virtue if there are several. The soul remains the seat of the good life, but the good is specified through function and activity, not through relation to a separate Form.

Aristotle’s ethical division of the soul is simpler and less mythic than Plato’s tripartite psychology. He distinguishes an irrational and a rational element, but the irrational itself is twofold. The vegetative power of nutrition and growth has no share in reason and lies outside specifically human excellence. The appetitive or desiring element, however, shares in reason “in a sense”: it can listen to reason, obey it, and be persuaded by it, as a child listens to a father. This is Aristotle’s preferred picture of moral psychology. Ethical formation is not chiefly the inner politics of three quasi-persons, but the training of desire into concord with reason.

It is characteristic that Aristotle even notes the vegetative power functions most in sleep, where goodness and badness are least manifest, except perhaps insofar as the dreams of better people are somewhat better. The remark is revealing. Moral excellence is not identical with mere life. It is a determination of desire, choice, and reason in activity. Ethical psychology is layered atop the biological functions without being reducible to them.

Because the appetitive soul can be trained, moral virtue is not in us by nature as a finished condition. Aristotle says it arises through habit: we become just by doing just acts, temperate by temperate acts, brave by brave acts. A virtue is a settled state concerned with choice, lying in a mean relative to us and determined by reason as the practically wise person would determine it. What one repeatedly does forms the very texture of one’s desires and character. In the Ethics, soul is not only what has faculties; it is what becomes good or bad through practice.

Book VI supplies the intellectual counterpart. Practical wisdom (phronēsis) is a true and reasoned capacity to act with respect to human goods. It is not mere theory of universals; it must know particulars, because action concerns the particular case. Choice is “desiderative reason” or “ratiocinative desire,” and good choice requires both right reason and right desire. Here Aristotle’s ethics and psychology meet exactly: practical thought does not move by itself; action arises where intellect and appetite converge on an end.

The same structure appears outside the Ethics in On the Motion of Animals. There Aristotle says animals move by intellect, imagination, purpose, wish, and appetite, all reducible to mind and desire, and he formulates the famous practical syllogism: “every man ought to walk; I am a man; straightway one walks.” This is not a formal curiosity. It shows that for Aristotle the soul is essentially an arc of cognition, desire, and movement. Ends appear; reason articulates them; desire moves the organism toward action.

This is why Aristotle can reject Socratic intellectualism without needing a Platonic myth of warring soul-substances. In Book VII he says Socrates was wrong to deny incontinence outright. The incontinent person does in one sense know, but not in the fully active way; passion puts him in a condition like sleep, drunkenness, or madness. Lower animals are not incontinent because they have no universal judgement, only imagination and memory of particulars. Weakness of soul is therefore a failure in the activation of practical knowledge under desire, not simple absence of knowledge.

Books VIII and IX add a quieter but important dimension. Aristotle calls the friend “another self,” and he argues that to perceive that we perceive and to think that we think is to be aware that we exist. Friendship extends this reflexive awareness outward: the friend’s being becomes lovable almost as one’s own. Soul here is no longer described only as the subject of isolated faculties or virtues; it is also the seat of shared life, mutual recognition, and social self-awareness.

Book X culminates in Aristotle’s highest claim about the soul. If happiness is activity in accordance with the highest virtue, it must be the activity of the best thing in us—reason, or whatever is our most divine element. That activity is contemplation. It is loved for its own sake, more continuous, more self-sufficient, more leisurely, and more akin to divine life than practical activity. Yet Aristotle does not deny embodiment here. The contemplative human still needs health, food, and external support, because our composite nature remains human even at its height. Contemplation crowns embodied life; it does not cancel it.

The end of the Ethics then returns from contemplation to formation. Argument alone does not remake everyone; the student’s soul must first be cultivated by habit for noble pleasure and noble aversion. Law and education must shape desire before reason can rule well. In that sense Aristotle remains close to Plato’s concern for psychic formation, but he explains that formation less by recollection and ascent than by habituation, law, and the social training of pleasure and pain.

4. Adjacent works across the corpus

Several adjacent treatises confirm the same picture. Metaphysics I traces human cognition upward from sensation to memory, from repeated memories to experience, and from experience to art and reasoned knowledge. That is a compact anti-Platonic epistemology: the road to universals begins with perception, not with recollection of a pre-bodily vision. Metaphysics VII, meanwhile, repeats that the soul of animals is the substance or form of the living being, so that bodily parts severed from the living whole are only equivocally what they were before.

Parts of Animals I states the same point in teleological idiom. The formal nature is more important than the material; a dead hand with the same shape is not really a hand because it can no longer perform its work. Hence the natural philosopher must study soul, or at least that part of soul that constitutes the essential character of the animal, because in animals soul is more truly nature than matter is. Aristotle’s biology is never merely materialist. It is a biology of form, function, and end.

Politics I transposes the same psychology into political analogy. In a passage embedded in a framework that also contains deeply objectionable hierarchies, Aristotle says that in the living creature soul naturally rules body, and intellect naturally rules appetite; equality between superior and inferior is harmful. Whatever one thinks of the political application, the psychic principle is clear: the good life requires rule by the better element. Aristotle and Plato are close here, though Aristotle states the relation more in terms of natural teleology than in the imagery of an inner city.

5. Aristotle’s development from Plato

On the side of continuity, Aristotle never abandons what might be called the Platonic seriousness of soul. Like the Plato report you attached, Aristotle treats the soul as what most decisively matters in a human being: it is the bearer of life, knowledge, desire, character, and happiness; the better element should rule; and the highest human activity has something divine about it. His nutritive doctrine even echoes Platonic aspiration when he says living things strive, as far as possible, to partake in the eternal and divine, and Book X of the Ethics speaks of reason or the best thing in us as divine or most divine.

The decisive difference, however, is ontological. Plato, as your attached report presents him, repeatedly treats soul as a self-moving and more readily separable principle that can be contrasted with body almost as inmate to prison, ruler to ruled, or charioteer to horses. Aristotle’s standard formula is different from the ground up: soul is the form and first actuality of a natural organized body. Even when he allows some separability for intellect, his normal question is not “how does soul inhabit body?” but “what organization makes this body alive as the kind of body it is?” Platonic dualism becomes Aristotelian hylomorphism.

A second difference concerns internal structure. Plato’s Republic, as summarized in your report, analyzes the human soul into rational, spirited, and appetitive parts for the sake of justice and psychic government. Aristotle usually begins instead from powers: nutritive, perceptive, appetitive, locomotive, intellective. Even in the Ethics, where he distinguishes rational and irrational elements, the appetitive part shares in reason mainly by obedience and persuasion, not by standing beside reason as a distinct ally like Platonic thumos. Aristotle’s account is more biological and developmental, less mythic and less architectonic in the political sense.

A third difference is epistemological. Plato often makes the soul know by recollecting what it encountered before embodiment. Aristotle instead says sensation gives rise to memory, repeated memories to experience, experience to art and science; and he explicitly roots memory in the primary sensory faculty and in time. Recollection becomes a human search through stored images and associations. His psychology of knowledge is thus continuous downward with animal perception rather than upward with a prenatal contemplation of Forms.

The fourth and perhaps sharpest difference concerns immortality. Plato’s strongest soul-texts, as your report presents them, tie ethics to the survival of the soul as the subject of post-mortem reward, punishment, or reincarnation. Aristotle does nothing comparable in the Ethics and almost nothing comparable in the Parva Naturalia. Only the active intellect in De Anima III receives the predicates “separable,” “immortal,” and “eternal,” while passive mind is destructible and memory is tied to the perishability of the sensory composite. Aristotle therefore leaves a genuine doctrine of the eternity of intellective actuality, but a far more austere and impersonal one than Plato’s.

Fifth, Aristotle’s evaluation of the body is much less starkly adversarial. He certainly subordinates body to soul, and he never lets bodily pleasure define the good life. Yet perception, memory, imagination, sleep, locomotion, and even ordinary thought all depend on bodily organs and processes. The body is not simply the obstacle to truth; it is the organized instrument through which the powers of soul are actualized. This is why Aristotle’s psychology generates zoology and physiology, whereas Plato’s greatest soul-dialogues more often generate metaphysics, ethics, and myth.

Even where Aristotle most clearly continues Platonic ethical concerns, he recasts them through his own ontology. He shares Plato’s conviction that the best life is rule by the better part and that the highest activity has affinity with the divine. But he rejects the separate Form of the Good, grounds happiness in human function, and makes virtue a state formed by habit and practical reason. He is therefore not anti-Platonic in the sense of abandoning concern for the soul; he is post-Platonic in the sense of translating that concern into the idiom of form, end, faculty, and embodied activity.

Conclusion

The most concise way to state Aristotle’s position is this. Soul is the actuality and form of a living body; the hierarchy of souls is the hierarchy of life-functions; most psychic states are affections of body and soul together; perception receives forms without matter; memory and recollection are temporal and imagistic; action arises from the conjunction of desire and practical reason; virtue is the good condition of a soul whose desires have been educated; and contemplation is the highest activity of the most divine thing in us. The whole corpus, not De Anima alone, is needed to see this fully. De Anima gives the ontology; the Parva Naturalia show the psychophysiology; the Nicomachean Ethics give the norm of a well-formed human soul.

That is Aristotle’s central development from Plato. ψυχή is still what is most decisive about life and human flourishing, but it is no longer primarily the traveler that may leave the body behind. It is the form of a living being, the source and end of its activities, the structure of its perception, desire, and thought, and—at its highest—the site where human life touches the divine through contemplation. Aristotle’s psychology is therefore at once biological, metaphysical, and ethical: a science of what it is to be alive, a theory of what it is to know and act, and an account of what it is for a human life to go well.

A natural next step would be a second version keyed much more densely to individual Bekker passages.
